Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
Discrete Mathematics > Graph Theory > Matchings, Marriage Lemma, Tutte's theorem
response_text:
We show that the answer is exactly **\(n\ge 3\)**.

### Necessity
If \(n<3\), there are fewer than 3 vertices, so no triangle exists at all. Hence condition (2) cannot hold. Thus \(n\ge 3\).

### Sufficiency
Assume \(n\ge 3\). We will color the \( \binom n2 \) edges/diagonals using the \(n\) colors so that every triangle has three different colors; then condition (2) is automatic.

Label the vertices \(1,2,\dots,n\) cyclically, and color the segment \(ij\) (\(i<j\)) by
\[
\text{color}(ij)= i+j \pmod n,
\]
using the \(n\) colors identified with residues mod \(n\).

Now consider any triangle with vertices \(a<b<c\). Its three side-colors are
\[
a+b,\quad b+c,\quad c+a \pmod n.
\]
If two of these were equal mod \(n\), then subtracting gives one of
\[
b-a,\ c-b,\ c-a
\]
congruent to \(0 \pmod n\), impossible because \(1\le b-a,\ c-b,\ c-a \le n-1\). So the three colors are distinct.

Therefore every triangle uses three distinct colors, so in particular for any three distinct colors, there is a triangle whose edges have exactly those three colors.

Hence such a coloring exists for every \(n\ge 3\).

Final answer: \(\boxed{n\ge 3}\)
